title: Scripture & Divine Revelation
summary: God has spoken. The sixty-six books of the Old and New Testaments are the inspired, inerrant, and sufficient Word of God — the only infallible rule for faith and life. All other claimed revelations are measured against this fixed standard.
scripture: 2 Timothy 3:16–17 — "All Scripture is breathed out by God and profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness."
detail:
The Westminster Confession opens with Scripture because epistemology precedes theology. Before we can speak rightly about God, we must establish how God has spoken to us. The doctrine of inspiration (theopneustos — God-breathed) affirms that the human authors wrote as carried along by the Holy Spirit, producing a text that is simultaneously fully human and fully authoritative. The canon is not a Catholic invention or an ecclesiastical power play — it is the church's formal recognition of what the Spirit had already marked as authoritative. The internal testimony of the Holy Spirit seals this conviction in the regenerate heart.

Islam's central charge against Christian Scripture is tahrif — the claim that the Bible has been systematically corrupted, making Muhammad's revelation necessary as a corrective. This claim is historically indefensible. The manuscript tradition for the New Testament is unmatched in ancient literature: over 5,800 Greek manuscripts, with the earliest fragments (P52, P66, P75) dating within decades of the originals — five centuries before the rise of Islam. No serious textual scholar, including secular critics, accepts the corruption thesis. More damaging still, the Quran itself repeatedly affirms the Torah and Gospel as valid and authoritative (Surah 5:47, 5:68, 10:94), instructing Muhammad to consult the People of the Book if in doubt. If those scriptures were already corrupted at the time of the Quran's composition, this instruction is theologically incoherent. The tahrif doctrine is not a historical finding — it is a post-hoc theological necessity invented to explain contradictions between the Quran and the scriptures it claims to succeed.

title: The Nature of God
summary: God is the only living and true God — infinite, eternal, and unchangeable in His being, wisdom, power, holiness, justice, goodness, and truth. He is not merely the greatest being in the universe. He is existence itself.
scripture: Exodus 3:14 — "God said to Moses, 'I AM WHO I AM.'"
detail:
The Westminster Confession (Ch. 2) draws on both Scripture and the classical theistic tradition to articulate a God of aseity — self-existence, complete independence from all contingent reality. God does not exist because of anything external to Himself. He is not one being among others, even the greatest. He is ipsum esse subsistens — being itself subsisting — as Aquinas formulated it. This metaphysical uniqueness grounds all the classical attributes: necessity, simplicity, immutability, eternity, omniscience, omnipotence, and omnibenevolence. Critically, this God is irreducibly personal: He speaks, covenants, loves, acts, and reveals. The union of infinite metaphysical power with genuine personal relationship is Christianity's most distinctive and scandalous claim.

Islam affirms a form of divine transcendence (tanzih) so radical that it severs God from meaningful personal relationship with creation. Allah's will is absolute and unconditioned — even His love and justice are not expressions of a stable nature but sovereign choices that could, in principle, be otherwise. This voluntarism — the doctrine that God's will precedes and determines His nature — produces a God whose goodness is definitionally whatever He wills rather than an expression of what He eternally is. Reformed theology insists that God's attributes are not arbitrary: He is not good because He chooses to be good, but because goodness flows necessarily from His eternal nature. The God of the Westminster Confession is not the God of Islamic theology. The difference is not superficial — it reaches to the metaphysical foundations of each system.

title: The Holy Trinity
summary: In the unity of the Godhead there are three Persons — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit — of one substance, power, and eternity. This is not tritheism. It is the only monotheism adequate to the God who is love.
scripture: Matthew 28:19 — "Baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit."
detail:
The Westminster Confession (Ch. 2.3) affirms that the one God eternally subsists in three Persons — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit — who are coequal, coeternal, and consubstantial. This doctrine did not emerge from Greek philosophical speculation imported into Christianity. It emerged from the early church's unavoidable encounter with the data: Jesus received worship, forgave sins, identified Himself with the Father, and sent the Spirit who is also called God. The Councils of Nicaea (325) and Constantinople (381) did not invent the Trinity — they articulated the grammar already implicit in apostolic proclamation. The Athanasian Creed formulates it with surgical precision: neither confounding the Persons nor dividing the substance.

Islam's charge of shirk — associating partners with God — is Christianity's most persistent external challenge and must be answered with precision, not embarrassment. The Islamic critique attacks tritheism, which is a heresy Christianity condemned before Islam existed. But the charge reveals a deeper misunderstanding: the Quran's own formulation of the Trinity (Surah 5:116) presents the three persons as Father, Jesus, and Mary — a configuration no Christian council, confession, or catechism has ever affirmed. Islam is refuting a position its source material invented. Furthermore, tawhid taken to its logical conclusion produces a God who was entirely alone before creation — which raises the question of how love can be an eternal divine attribute if there was no object of love until the universe was made. The Trinity resolves this: love, self-giving, and communion are not created realities that God later participated in. They are eternal realities within the Godhead itself. The God of the Bible did not learn to love when He made the world. He always was love — because He always was Trinity.

title: The Covenant of Grace
summary: Having fallen in Adam, humanity stands under just condemnation. God, not humanity, initiates rescue. The entire arc of Scripture — from Abraham to Christ — is one covenant of grace, fulfilled in the obedience and sacrifice of the Son.
scripture: Genesis 3:15 — "I will put enmity between you and the woman, and between your offspring and her offspring; he shall bruise your head."
detail:
The Westminster Confession's covenant theology (Ch. 7) is one of Reformed theology's most distinctive and underappreciated contributions. After the fall, God did not abandon humanity to its just desserts — He established a covenant of grace, promising a Redeemer. This covenant was administered differently across redemptive history — through Noah, Abraham, Moses, and David — but was always one covenant, one promise, one Christ. The entire Old Testament is not a failed experiment replaced by a better one. It is a single unfolding narrative whose terminus is Jesus of Nazareth, in whom every type, shadow, and prophecy finds its substance. Salvation has always been by grace through faith — Abraham believed God, and it was counted to him as righteousness (Romans 4:3).

Islam has no covenant theology and no doctrine of grace in the Reformed sense. Salvation in Islam is fundamentally transactional: righteous deeds weighed against sins on the Day of Judgment, with Allah's mercy as an additional factor that remains sovereign and unpredictable. There is no assurance of salvation, no imputed righteousness, no federal headship, and no atoning mediator. The Islamic believer cannot know, in this life, whether the scales will tip in their favor. Reformed theology, by contrast, grounds assurance not in the believer's performance but in the covenant-keeping faithfulness of God. The elect are not saved because they believed well enough — they are saved because the eternal Son fulfilled the covenant of redemption on their behalf, and the Spirit applies that work with sovereign efficacy. These are not variations on the same theme. They are structurally incompatible accounts of the human problem and its solution.

title: The Incarnation of the Son
summary: The eternal Son of God took on a true human nature — body and rational soul — in the womb of the Virgin Mary. He is one Person in two natures: fully divine, fully human, without confusion, change, division, or separation.
scripture: John 1:14 — "And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we have seen his glory."
detail:
The Westminster Confession (Ch. 8.2) follows Chalcedon (451 AD) in affirming that the Son of God took to Himself a true and complete human nature — not a divine being dressed in a human costume, not a human being elevated to divine status, but one undivided Person in whom two natures are united without either being diminished or confused. This is the hypostatic union: the most philosophically demanding doctrine in Christian theology, and the one that makes atonement possible. Only God can bear the infinite weight of sin's penalty. Only a human being can stand in humanity's place. The God-man alone satisfies both requirements.

Islam categorically denies the Incarnation. The Quran teaches that Jesus (Isa) was a prophet and messenger — born of a virgin, performing miracles, and highly honored — but not divine. The idea that God would take on human flesh is, in Islamic theology, a category error bordering on blasphemy: it confuses the Creator with the creature in a way that compromises divine transcendence. The Reformed response is that the Incarnation does not compromise divine transcendence — it demonstrates it. A God so sovereignly free that He can enter His own creation without ceasing to be God is more transcendent, not less, than a God whose transcendence requires Him to remain at an infinite remove. Furthermore, Islam's elevation of Jesus as prophet while denying His crucifixion and resurrection removes the only mechanism by which His life could accomplish anything for human salvation. A prophet who did not die for sin and did not rise from the dead is an inspiring figure — but he cannot save. The Incarnation is not theological excess. It is the precise mechanism by which a holy God rescues guilty humanity without compromising His justice.

title: The Resurrection of Christ
summary: On the third day He rose bodily from the dead — the same body that was crucified, now glorified and transformed. This is not myth, metaphor, or spiritual symbol. It is the central claim of the Christian faith, historically documented and theologically indispensable.
scripture: 1 Corinthians 15:17 — "If Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile and you are still in your sins."
detail:
Paul's logic in 1 Corinthians 15 is uncompromising: the Resurrection is not one doctrine among many that can be reinterpreted or demythologized without loss. It is the load-bearing arch of the entire Christian structure. Remove it and everything collapses — atonement, justification, assurance, future hope. The Westminster Confession (Ch. 8.4) affirms that Christ rose again on the third day with the same body in which He suffered, having satisfied divine justice through His obedient life and substitutionary death, and that His resurrection secures the justification of all whom He represents. The minimal facts argument — accepted by the majority of critical historians regardless of theological commitment — establishes five bedrock historical data points: Jesus died by crucifixion, was buried in a known tomb, the tomb was found empty, multiple individuals and groups experienced post-mortem appearances, and the disciples were transformed from defeated followers into fearless martyrs. The Resurrection is the hypothesis that best explains all five simultaneously.

Islam's position is stark: the Quran explicitly states that Jesus was not crucified and did not die — 'it was made to appear so' (Surah 4:157). This single verse places Islam in direct conflict with the most well-attested event of ancient history. The crucifixion of Jesus under Pontius Pilate is affirmed by Tacitus, Josephus, the Babylonian Talmud, and every strand of the New Testament tradition — sources with no motive to invent a shameful execution. The substitution theory — that God replaced Jesus with another person — creates an additional theological problem: it means God actively deceived the disciples and the entire early church into worshipping a risen Christ who never died and never rose. This is not divine mercy. It is divine deception on a cosmic scale — which raises fundamental questions about the character of the God Islam claims is perfectly truthful. The Reformed Christian does not merely assert the Resurrection as a faith commitment. He presents it as the best-evidenced historical claim in the ancient world and invites honest historical examination.
